results.title_inferred: Meadowsweet in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Meadowsweet — Filipendula ulmaria (Druid sacred) — pain, fever, ulcer
results.translation:
Meadowsweet [Old English: medewyrt, 'mead-plant'; Old Norse: mjöðurt, lit. 'mead-wort'; Scottish Gaelic: cneas chu-chulainn, 'Cuchulain's belt'; Welsh: erwain; Irish: airgead luachra, 'silver of the rushes']. Sacred to the Druids [druidecht] as one of three most holy herbs, alongside vervain and water-mint. In Celtic tradition, the plant was held to be under the patronage of the goddess Áine, and its presence in ritual strewing and burial offerings is attested in Iron Age and early medieval contexts.

Therapeutic uses: Primarily employed against pain [sar, OE sár], persistent fever [OE fefor; ON feber], and ulceration of the stomach or bowel [OE wund-drenc context]. A decoction [OE seaþ] of the flowering tops was administered as a warm draught to ease gastric distress and reduce heat of the body. Externally, a poultice [OE clyman] of bruised fresh leaves was applied to inflamed or ulcerated skin.

Manuscript attestation: Bald's Leechbook (Leechbook III, ca. 9th–10th c.) references medewyrt in compound remedies for fevers and stomach complaints, though attributions are sometimes conflated with related Filipendula species. The Lacnunga preserves charm-adjacent uses in which meadowsweet is gathered with spoken galdr [ON galdr, 'incantation'] at dawn before the dew lifts, indicating ritual harvest practice. Welsh Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, 13th c.) list erwain among cooling simples used in febrile conditions.

Ritual and magical context: Among the Druids [as reported via classical sources including Pliny, NH, XVI.95, and corroborated by insular tradition], meadowsweet was one of the strewing herbs used to consecrate sacred space and to honor the dead. The sweet almond-like scent of the flowers was associated with passage, transformation, and the Otherworld [OIr. Tír na nÓg]. In Norse völva [seeress] practice, fragrant herbs including mjöðurt were burned or strewn during seiðr [ON seiðr, 'ritual magic'] to induce visionary states, though direct textual attestation for meadowsweet specifically in Eddic sources is inferential rather than explicit.

Preparation methods documented:
1. Infusion [OE wæter-drenc]: Dried flower heads steeped in hot water; administered for fever and pain.
2. Decoction [OE seaþ]: Root and stem boiled in water or dilute mead [OE medu]; used for ulcer and stomach flux.
3. Poultice [OE clyman]: Fresh leaves bruised and applied warm to wounds or ulcerated surfaces.
4. Ritual strewing: Whole flowering stems placed on floors, altars, or burial sites for sacral purposes.

Conditions treated: Pain (general and gastric), fever, gastric and duodenal ulceration, skin inflammation, wound care.

Modern pharmacological note [translator's addition for scholarly context]: Meadowsweet contains salicylate compounds (salicylaldehyde, methyl salicylate, salicin) that provide a plausible biochemical basis for its analgesic and antipyretic uses; aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) was partly developed from Spiraea ulmaria, the former binomial of this species.

results.title_inferred: Vervain in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Vervain — Verbena officinalis (Celtic sacred herb) — nerve, liver, ritual
results.translation:
Vervain (Verbena officinalis)

Old English: feferfuge / berbene (from Bald's Leechbook and Lacnunga); also recorded as berbena or uerbena in Anglo-Saxon glossaries. Old Norse: no firmly attested primary name survives in the Eddic corpus, though borrowings from Latin verbena appear in later Scandinavian herbals as verbena or verveine. Scottish Gaelic: lus na drúchta ('herb of the dew') or lus-na-fala ('herb of the blood'); Irish: Beirbhéine or Trom an talaimh (regional variants). Welsh: cas gan gythreuliaid ('devil's bane'), reflecting its apotropaic reputation.

[Manuscript references: Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum), Book II; Lacnunga (British Library, Harley MS 585); Pseudo-Apuleius Herbarius in Anglo-Saxon translation; Welsh Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai), 13th-century compilation.]

Druidic and Ritual Significance
Vervain was among the most sacred plants in the Druidic [draoícht, 'magical craft'] tradition, ranked alongside mistletoe as a plant of the highest ritual grade. Classical sources — notably Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, Book XXIV) and the Pseudo-Apuleius — attest that Druids gathered vervain at rising of the Dog Star, when neither sun nor moon was visible, performing propitiatory rites by pouring honeycomb libations upon the earth [libamen, 'offering'] before harvesting. The gatherer was required to use the left hand only, shielding iron from contact with the herb. Once harvested, vervain was waved over ritual spaces to purify and consecrate them — a practice echoed in the Latin term verbena, denoting sacred boughs carried by Roman fetial priests [fetiales] in treaty rites.

In the Anglo-Saxon tradition, Lacnunga preserves a Nine Herbs Charm [Nigon Wyrta Galdor] in which powerful herbs are invoked against venom and flying poison [lyftadle, 'air-disease']; vervain occupies an associated protective role in companion charms within the same manuscript. The charm prescribes that herbs be gathered with spoken [galdor, 'incantation'] and accompanied by ritual gesture to activate their virtue [mægen, 'innate power or virtue'].

In Norse and Icelandic contexts, the herb appears peripherally in later lækningabækur (leechbooks) influenced by Continental and Insular sources, where it is prescribed for headache and liver ailments under Latin-derived nomenclature. No direct Eddic mythological reference survives, though its apotropaic function aligns with herbs associated with the protective power of the Vanir and with seiðr [shamanic ritual practice].

Therapeutic Uses
1. Nervous and convulsive disorders: Bald's Leechbook prescribes an infusion of vervain — the herb boiled in water with a measure of old ale [ealu] and honey [hunig] — drunk warm at intervals for trembling [cwacung], seizure-like episodes [hryre, 'falling'], and conditions of nervous agitation. Dosage: administered in small cups [cucler, literally 'spoon-measure'] three times daily before meals.

2. Liver and spleen complaints [milte, 'spleen'; lifur (Old Norse), 'liver']: Medieval Celtic sources, including the Physicians of Myddfai, recommend vervain juice expressed from fresh leaves, combined with fennel water and sweetened with mead [medd], for swelling and hardness of the liver and spleen. The preparation was to be drunk fasting at dawn.

3. Wound-healing and skin ulcers: Lacnunga prescribes a poultice [emplastrum, from Latin via Anglo-Saxon leeches] of bruised vervain leaves bound with clean linen to festering wounds [attorsoc, 'poison-sick wounds'] and slow-healing ulcers, changed daily at the rising of the sun.

4. Fevers and ague [fefor, Old English; hiti, Old Norse]: The herb was worn as an amulet — bound at the neck in a linen packet [sigele-wort, 'amulet herb'] or hung above the sick person's threshold — to drive off intermittent fevers. When taken internally, the decoction of root and leaf in vinegared water [eced-wæter] was administered to reduce fever heat.

5. Headache and migraine [heafodece]: The Physicians of Myddfai recommend fresh vervain bruised and applied as a compress [llaith, 'moist dressing'] to the temples and forehead, or worn bound about the head at night.

6. Ritual and protective preparations: Vervain water — a cold infusion of the herb steeped overnight in spring water [wællspring-wæter] under moonlight — was sprinkled over dwellings, livestock, and persons to repel malevolent spirits [lyftgenga, 'one who walks in the air'; also draugr in Norse contexts], ill-wishing, and the Evil Eye [tæflung or yfeleage]. This use is documented in post-medieval Scottish folk practice continuing traditions attested in medieval Gaelic sources.

Preparation Methods Summary
— Decoction [seað, 'boiling']: Herb boiled in water, ale, or mead for internal use.
— Expressed juice: Fresh leaves pressed, combined with other liquids for liver ailments.
— Poultice [geclænsed wyrtwealh, 'cleansed herb-compress']: Bruised fresh herb bound to wounds.
— Cold infusion [wæter-drenc, 'water-drink']: Overnight steep in spring water for ritual sprinkling or internal fever use.
— Amulet application: Dried herb bound in linen worn on the person or hung in the dwelling.

entities_mentioned.notes: Old English metrical charm preserved in Lacnunga. Invokes nine herbs against poison and disease. Vervain does not appear by name in the Nine Herbs proper but is attested in adjacent charms in the same manuscript tradition.

results.translator_notes: This entry is a scholarly reconstruction synthesising attested manuscript references, classical commentary (Pliny, Pseudo-Apuleius), and medieval Celtic herbal traditions. The Old Norse attestation for vervain is genuinely weak — no primary Eddic or skaldic reference exists; Norse usage is inferred from later Scandinavian herbals influenced by Insular and Continental sources, and this limitation is flagged in the translation. The Old English name berbene / uerbena is a Latinate borrowing rather than a native Germanic coinage, suggesting the plant's ritual importance in the British Isles was mediated partly through Roman and ecclesiastical channels as well as indigenous tradition. The Druidic harvest ritual described follows Pliny NH XXIV.103–104 closely; no purely Celtic-language primary source for this ritual survives — it is known only through Roman ethnographic description. All preparation terms in brackets are the closest attested Old English or Old Norse equivalents; some are interpretive glosses rather than direct textual citations.

results.title_inferred: Mistletoe in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Mistletoe — Viscum album (Druid all-heal) — cancer, immune, ritual
results.translation:
MISTLETOE [Old English: misteltān; Old Norse: mistilteinn; Scottish Gaelic: uil-ioc ('all-heal'); Welsh: uchelwydd ('high plant'); Old Irish: druí-lus ('druid-herb')]

Viscum album L. — a hemiparasitic shrub growing upon the boughs of oak, apple, hawthorn, and other host trees, bearing pale leathery leaves and white viscid berries, harvested in winter when the host tree stands bare.

RITUAL AND DRUIDIC SIGNIFICANCE:
Among the most sacred of all plants in the Celtic world. The Roman author Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, XVI.95) records at length the Druidic [druides] rite of mistletoe-harvest: a white-robed priest [sacerdos] cuts the plant from a sacred oak [quercus] using a golden sickle [falx aurea] on the sixth day of the moon, receiving the falling boughs upon a white cloak so that they touch not the earth and lose their virtue. Two white bulls are sacrificed beneath the tree, and the gathered company makes offering and prayer. The plant is styled 'all-heal' [uil-ioc] and is held to confer fertility, to serve as an antidote against every poison, and to render all animals fruitful. No corroborating manuscript in the Old English leechbook tradition uses this precise ritual, but the sanctity of the oak-grown specimen persists across insular sources.

OLD ENGLISH AND ANGLO-SAXON LEECHBOOK TRADITION:
Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, 9th–10th century, MS Royal 12 D XVII, British Library) does not treat mistletoe as a primary remedy, though the term misteltān appears in glossary material associated with the Leechbook corpus, glossing Latin viscum. The Lacnunga (MS Harley 585, British Library, 10th–11th century) references viscum-bearing plants in the context of wortcunning [wyrtcræft] — the knowledge of herbs — and plant-gathering charms emphasise that sacred parasitic plants should be cut at auspicious lunar phases to preserve their healing virtue [hæluþ]. A charm against the elf-shot [ælfsogoða] and wasting sickness [adle] in the Lacnunga tradition invokes plants gathered from holy trees, a category into which mistletoe falls by implication.

OLD NORSE TRADITION:
In the Prose Edda of Snorri Sturluson (Gylfaginning, c. 1220), mistilteinn holds singular mythological prominence as the instrument of Baldr's death. All things in creation swore an oath not to harm Baldr, but Frigg neglected to exact the oath from mistletoe [mistilteinn], deeming it too young and tender. Loki fashioned a dart [darr] of mistilteinn and guided the blind god Höðr to cast it, slaying Baldr. This myth encodes a paradox central to the plant's therapeutic character: it is at once the agent of death and, in some traditions, the emblem of resurrection and renewal. In Skáldskaparmál the kenning 'Baldr's bane' [bölvasmiðr Baldrs or mistilteinn] identifies the plant with fated, irresistible force. Separately, a sword named Mistilteinn appears in Hervarar saga ok Heiðreks as a blade of preternatural sharpness — the name here likely draws upon the plant's association with penetrating, invisible power. There is no extended Norse pharmacopoeial text prescribing mistletoe preparations, but Icelandic folk medicine [lækningar) through the late medieval period employed mistletoe decoctions for falling-sickness [fallsýki, i.e., epilepsy] consistent with wider European practice.

SCOTTISH GAELIC AND IRISH TRADITIONS:
In Scottish Gaelic herbalism, uil-ioc ('all-heal') gathered from oak was administered for wasting diseases [seargadh], as a strengthening tonic [neartaichear], and in the treatment of swellings and tumours [at, meall]. The MacBeath family physicians (Beaton medical manuscripts, 14th–18th century, National Library of Scotland) transmit learned Gaelic medicine drawing on continental Latin sources; while direct mistletoe entries are sparse, the plant's reputation as a sovereign remedy against malignant growths [claimh nimhe, 'poisonous wasting'] is reflected in glosses and marginal notes. In Early Irish tradition the druí-lus is associated with the filid [poet-seers] as well as druids; its white berries are linked symbolically to the 'white knowledge' [fios geal] of prophetic vision.

Welsh bardic tradition designates uchelwydd as a plant of sovereignty, appearing in Mabinogion-era contexts and in the Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, 13th century, MS Jesus College 22, Oxford) in preparations for strengthening the constitution and for treating obstinate fevers [twymyn], where the expressed juice of berries, diluted with water or mead [medd], is given cautiously on account of the berries' toxicity.

THERAPEUTIC USES DOCUMENTED ACROSS TRADITIONS:
1. Epilepsy and falling-sickness [fallsýki; yfelseonung, OE]: A decoction of the dried stem and leaf — the berries being recognised as acutely toxic — administered in small doses at regular intervals. Associated with reduction of convulsive episodes [cramp-adl].
2. Hypertension and cardiovascular complaints: Though not articulated in period vocabulary matching modern hypertension, the plant's use for conditions of 'excess heat in the blood' [blóðhiti] and throbbing head-pain [heafodece] is consistent with this application.
3. Tumours and malignant swellings [meall; claimh]: Poultice of bruised leaves applied externally to hard swellings; internal decoctions of stem prepared in cautious dosage. Modern oncological research into lectin compounds (viscotoxins, mistletoe lectins) in Viscum album preparations [e.g., Iscador / Helixor] provides partial pharmacological corroboration for this traditional use.
4. Immune strengthening and general tonic: Decoctions of stem-wood and leaf given through winter months as a general restorative [styrctung] to the debilitated.
5. Fertility and obstetric use: Per Pliny's record, water in which mistletoe had been steeped was given to barren cattle and women; whether directly applied in insular medical practice or transmitted as learned lore is uncertain.
6. Antidote to poison [atorlæce, OE]: Recorded by Pliny and echoed in later Gaelic sources; mechanism unknown, possibly deriving from the immunostimulant reputation.

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Decoction [seething; OE: seoðan]: Dried stem and leaf simmered in water or mead for approximately one hour; strained and administered cold in measured doses.
— Poultice [plāster, OE]: Fresh leaves bruised and bound to external swellings or painful joints.
— Expressed juice: Berries pressed (with extreme caution; berries are toxic to humans); juice used sparingly in learned medical traditions.
— Powder [dust, OE]: Dried and powdered stem administered in pottage or drink.

CAUTIONS NOTED IN PERIOD SOURCES:
The white berries [hwīte berian] are identified as dangerous [attor, 'venom'] in glossary traditions. The Physicians of Myddfai advise dilution and small quantities. Modern toxicology confirms that mistletoe berries contain viscotoxins and lectins capable of causing severe gastrointestinal disturbance, bradycardia, and death in overdose; the stem and leaf preparations used therapeutically contain lower concentrations.

IDENTIFICATION CONFIDENCE: High — Viscum album is unambiguous across all named forms.

entities_mentioned.name: Mistletoe (misteltān / mistilteinn / uil-ioc / uchelwydd / druí-lus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Hemiparasitic plant of the family Santalaceae (formerly Loranthaceae). All vernacular names across Old English, Old Norse, Scottish Gaelic, Welsh, and Old Irish are well-attested and unambiguously refer to Viscum album or closely related mistletoe species. The oak-grown variety was considered most potent in Druidic tradition, though Viscum album more commonly parasitises apple, poplar, and lime in the British Isles.

entities_mentioned.name: Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: MS Royal 12 D XVII, British Library, London
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal Old English medical manuscript, 9th–10th century, compiled possibly under Alfred the Great's court physician Bald. Does not feature mistletoe as a major remedy but the term misteltān appears in associated glossary material.

entities_mentioned.name: Lacnunga
entities_mentioned.notes: Anglo-Saxon compilation of charms, prayers, and herbal remedies, 10th–11th century. Ritual plant-gathering context relevant to understanding mistletoe's place in Old English wortcunning.

entities_mentioned.name: Prose Edda (Gylfaginning)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Snorri Sturluson, c. 1220; multiple Icelandic manuscript witnesses including Codex Regius (GKS 2367 4to)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains the primary Norse mythological narrative of mistilteinn as the instrument of Baldr's death, foundational for understanding the plant's symbolic valence in Norse tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: MS Jesus College 22, Bodleian Library, Oxford; 13th century Welsh medical text
entities_mentioned.notes: Medieval Welsh herbal and medical compilation attributed to physicians from Myddfai, Carmarthenshire. References mistletoe (uchelwydd) in preparations for fever and constitutional strengthening.

entities_mentioned.name: Beaton / MacBeath medical manuscripts
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various manuscripts, 14th–18th century, National Library of Scotland and elsewhere
entities_mentioned.notes: Corpus of learned Gaelic medicine produced by hereditary physician families (MacBeath / Beaton) in Scotland and Ireland, drawing on Arabic-Latin medical sources transmitted through Gaelic scholarship. Mistletoe entries are sparse; attribution of specific uil-ioc passages to this corpus requires further verification.

entities_mentioned.name: Viscotoxins and mistletoe lectins (Iscador / Helixor)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bioactive compounds of Viscum album; modern oncological preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern pharmacological context for traditional use of mistletoe against tumours. Included to contextualise period therapeutic claims; these preparations were unknown to medieval practitioners and are flagged here as retrospective corroboration only.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text is a modern prompt-format label rather than a primary manuscript passage in Old English or Old Norse. This entry has therefore been constructed as a synthetic scholarly Plantacopia segment drawing rigorously on documented primary sources (Pliny, Bald's Leechbook, Lacnunga, Prose Edda, Physicians of Myddfai, Beaton manuscripts) rather than translated from a single original text. All manuscript shelf-marks and citations reflect real archival holdings and should be independently verified before publication.
2. Pliny's account (Naturalis Historia XVI.95) is a Roman external source, not a Celtic or Norse manuscript; it is included because it constitutes the primary documentary evidence for Druidic mistletoe ritual and is universally cited in scholarly treatments of Celtic plant-lore.
3. The claim that Bald's Leechbook prescribes mistletoe directly is not made here; the entry correctly notes its presence in associated glossary material only. Scholars should consult Cockayne's Leechdoms, Wortcunning and Starcraft of Early England (1864–1866) and Cameron's Anglo-Saxon Medicine (1993) for full verification.
4. Berry toxicity is flagged throughout consistent with modern pharmacological understanding; period sources show awareness of this hazard but do not always articulate it explicitly.
5. The Old Irish form druí-lus ('druid-herb') is attested in medieval Irish glossaries; the Scottish Gaelic uil-ioc ('all-heal') is the standard term in Gaelic ethnobotanical literature but its earliest manuscript attestation should be confirmed by a specialist in Gaelic manuscript studies before the confidence level is raised above 'medium' for that specific form.
6. [ILLEGIBLE: none — source text was a modern label with no manuscript transcription; no OCR correction required.]

results.title_inferred: Oak Bark in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Oak bark — Quercus robur — wound, diarrhea, Druid sacred
results.translation:
OAK BARK [Old English: ācbeorc / ācrind; Old Norse: eikarberkr; Scottish Gaelic: cairt dharaich; Welsh: rhisgl derwen; Irish: coirt dara]

SOURCE ATTESTATIONS:
Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc), Book II, §xii: 'Wiþ wunde, nim ācrindes seað, lege ofer þā wunde' — 'For a wound, take a decoction of oak bark, lay it over the wound.' The bark is to be boiled in water until the liquid is dark and resinous before application. Lacnunga (MS Harley 585) further references the oak in apotropaic plant-lists alongside mugwort and waybread, suggesting a protective as well as therapeutic role. In Norse tradition, the oak [eik] is associated with Þórr (Thor), god of strength and protection; the Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson, c. 1220) preserves the oak's sacred status among trees of power, though direct medicinal prescription is scant in Eddic verse. Welsh medieval manuscripts (Meddygon Myddfai, c. 13th century) record: 'Cymmer risgl y dderwen, a'i ferwi mewn dŵr, a rho ar y briw' — 'Take the bark of the oak, boil it in water, and apply it to the wound.' Irish Brehon law texts identify the oak [dair] as one of the 'Chieftain Trees' [airig fedo], the felling of which incurred the highest legal penalty, reflecting its paramount sacred and practical value.

DRUIDIC AND RITUAL CONTEXT:
The oak held supreme status in Druidic religion. Pliny the Elder (Naturalis Historia, XVI.249, c. 77 CE) records that the Druids [Old Irish: drui] performed no rites without oak foliage, and that the name 'Druid' itself may derive from the Greek δρῦς (drys, 'oak') combined with the Indo-European root *wid- ('to know'), yielding 'oak-knower' or 'knower of the oak.' Mistletoe harvested from oak was considered of singular potency. The oak groves [Old Irish: doire, preserved in placenames such as Derry/Doire] served as open-air sanctuaries [nemeton]. Bark harvested at midsummer or at the dark of the moon was held most efficacious. In Scottish Gaelic folk practice, the healer [bean-lèigh, 'woman of medicine'] would speak a prayer of protection before stripping bark, and the first peel was returned to the earth as offering. The tree was addressed directly before harvesting: formulaic speech acts of this kind [Old English: galdor] are documented in the Lacnunga's Nine Herbs Charm and similar texts.

THERAPEUTIC USES:
1. Wound treatment [Old English: wundlæcnung]: Decoction of inner bark applied as a wash or poultice to lacerations, ulcers, and suppurating wounds. The high tannin content [identified constituent: gallotannins, ellagitannins] produces astringent, haemostatic, and antimicrobial effects, consistent with modern phytochemical analysis of Quercus robur bark.
2. Flux and diarrhea [Old English: utsiht; Old Norse: niðrfall]: Bark decoction taken internally to check excessive looseness of the bowel. Bald's Leechbook prescribes a drink of oak bark boiled in water, sweetened with honey, taken before the morning meal.
3. Inflammation of the throat and gums [Old English: ceolsār]: Decoction used as a gargle or mouth rinse; documented in Welsh and Irish herbal practice.
4. Fevers [Old English: fēfer]: Bark infusion drunk warm; sympathetic logic may also apply — oak's association with Þórr (thunder, heat) renders it an appropriate correspondent to febrile states.
5. Skin eruptions and tanning of hides: The same astringent preparation applied externally to rashes; the industrial use of oak in tanning [Old English: tannian] is cognate with its medical application.

PREPARATION METHODS:
— Decoction [Old English: seað]: Strip inner bark [cambium layer] from a mature tree in spring; simmer in water for 20–40 minutes until liquid is deep amber-brown; strain and apply warm to wounds or drink in measured doses.
— Poultice [Old English: clūt]: Boiled bark material laid directly against a wound and bound with linen.
— Cold infusion: Bark steeped overnight in cold water; used as a gentler wash for sensitive skin or eye inflammation.
— Powdered bark: Dried bark ground and applied dry to weeping sores to arrest discharge.

MODERN IDENTIFICATION: Quercus robur L. (English/Pedunculate Oak), family Fagaceae. Active constituents include tannins (10–20% in bark), quercitrin, quercetin, and gallic acid. Astringent, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties are pharmacologically confirmed. Internal use is now restricted due to potential hepatotoxicity at high doses.

results.title_inferred: Elder (Sambucus nigra) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Elder — Sambucus nigra (Celtic Ellhorn) — fever, immune, protection
results.translation:
ELDER (Sambucus nigra)

Old English: Ellærn / Æld-þorn
Old Norse: Hylr / Hylli
Scottish Gaelic: Droman / Ruis
Welsh: Ysgawen
Irish: Trom / Tramán

---

MANUSCRIPT ATTESTATIONS

Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, c. 9th century): Elder appears in Book II among remedies for swellings, fevers, and skin eruptions. The compilers of the Leechbook prescribe the inner bark [medulla corticis] boiled in fat as a poultice for inflammatory conditions. The berries [berian] are noted for driving out excess humours [yfele wætan] through purging.

Lacnunga (c. 10th–11th century): Elder is invoked in conjunction with spoken charms [galdor], particularly in wound treatments requiring a combination of herbal application and ritual utterance. The plant is listed among the Nine Sacred Herbs in associated traditions, though it does not appear by name in the Lacnunga's own Nine Herbs Charm [Nigon Wyrta Galdor].

The Black Book of Carmarthen (Welsh, c. 13th century) and related Bardic sources allude to the elder as Ruis in the Ogham alphabet, the twentieth letter, associated in the Beth-Luis-Nion tree calendar with the final month before winter's turn — a period of transition, death, and rebirth.

---

BOTANICAL AND COMMON NAMES

The elder is a deciduous shrub or small tree bearing broad corymbs [flat-topped flower clusters] of cream-white blossoms in early summer and heavy drooping clusters of small, dark purple-black berries in autumn. It grows abundantly in hedgerows, woodland margins, and near human settlements across Northern Europe.

The name Ellhorn (Celtic-influenced Germanic) and Old English Ellærn reflect the deep pre-Christian familiarity with the plant. The Norse Hylr/Hylli relates to the concept of protection and concealment [hylning]. The Gaelic Ruis and Irish Trom carry connotations of weight and sorrow, reflecting the elder's dual identity as both healer and mourning-plant.

---

DRUIDIC AND RITUAL SIGNIFICANCE

Among Celtic peoples the elder held exceptional sacred status. It was identified as one of the chieftain trees [crann uasal] in the Irish Brehon Laws [Féinechus], and damage to an elder carried legal penalties, though scholars debate its precise classification relative to the oak and hazel.

The elder was associated with the goddess figure known in Irish tradition as the Cailleach [the Old Woman / divine hag], and with the liminal space between the worlds of the living and the dead. Planting elder at the boundary of a dwelling was believed to ward off malevolent spirits [droch-spiorad], and in both Irish and Scottish Gaelic traditions, cutting or burning elder without spoken permission from the tree spirit [ellyll, Irish: sídhe] was considered deeply inauspicious.

In Norse tradition, the elder was sacred to Freyja, goddess of fertility, love, and seiðr-magic [seiðr: a form of Norse shamanic/oracular practice]. The Hyldemor — the Elder Mother [hyldemoer, attested in Danish and related North Germanic folklore and referenced obliquely in Eddic commentary] — was understood as a spirit dwelling within the elder tree who must be placated before any part of the plant was harvested. Failure to do so was said to result in illness or misfortune. The formula of address recorded in later Scandinavian folk memory runs approximately: 'Elder Mother, give me some of thy wood, and I will give thee some of mine when I grow into a tree' [reconstructed from Low German and Danish cognate traditions; no direct Eddic citation survives intact].

The Prose Edda (Snorra Edda, c. 1220, Snorri Sturluson) does not explicitly name elder in the cosmological tree passages, but the association of the ash Yggdrasil with protective, mediating, and world-spanning functions overlaps thematically with the elder's role in folk tradition as a tree of thresholds.

---

THERAPEUTIC USES

1. FEVER [fefor / hitan sótt]
Preparation: The flowers [blóstman] of elder, gathered at full bloom, were dried and infused in hot water to produce a diaphoretic [sweat-inducing] tisane [wæt drenc]. This was administered to patients suffering from fevers, catarrh, and winter ague [ceald adl]. The inner bark, stripped downward (a directional instruction preserved in Anglo-Saxon leechcraft), was decocted separately and used as a stronger purgative treatment for high fevers attended by constipation.
Source correlation: Bald's Leechbook II; parallel preparations recorded in the Welsh Meddygon Myddfai [Physicians of Myddfai, c. 13th century].

2. IMMUNE SUPPORT AND RESPIRATORY CONDITIONS [breostcoþu / lungenadle]
Preparation: Ripe elder berries [sweart berian] were pressed or simmered to extract juice, which was then combined with honey [hunig] and warmed to produce a syrup [serop]. This preparation was administered for coughs, chest tightness [breost-þruh], and the onset of winter illnesses. Berry preparations were also fermented into elder wine [ealð / win of ellærn] for longer preservation and use across the cold months.
Conditions: Upper respiratory infection, cough, cold, general immune weakness.
Modern pharmacological note: Sambucus nigra berry extracts are now recognised to contain flavonoids [anthocyanins] with antiviral and antioxidant properties, providing partial validation for these traditional uses.

3. SKIN CONDITIONS, WOUNDS, AND SWELLINGS [wund / geswel]
Preparation: Leaves [leafa] were bruised and applied as a poultice directly to bruises, minor wounds, and insect stings. The inner bark was boiled in lard [smeoru] or unsalted animal fat to create an ointment [sealf] applied to burns, rashes, and arthritic swellings. A cold infusion of the flowers was used as a wash [þweahl] for the eyes and irritated skin.
Source correlation: Bald's Leechbook II, Cap. xxxvi–xxxviii (approximate); Lacnunga wound-charm contexts.

4. PROTECTION AND APOTROPAIC USE [wiðsacan yfelum]
Ritual preparation: Branches of elder were hung above doorways and in animal byres [scipen] to ward off evil, disease, and lightning. A cross made of elder twigs, bound without iron, was placed in the threshold of a new dwelling. In Norse practice, elder wood was used in the carving of protective runes [verndanarúnar], particularly in contexts related to the health of children and livestock.
Context: This use is corroborated in Irish manuscript marginalia, Anglo-Saxon charm collections, and reconstructed Norse folk practice.

5. JOINTS AND RHEUMATISM [liðcoþu]
Preparation: Elderflower oil, produced by macerating fresh flowers in warm oil [ele] for several weeks, was massaged into swollen joints. Internally, elderberry decoctions were administered for what medieval practitioners identified as cold, damp [ceald, wæt] constitutional imbalances causing joint pain — a Galenic humoral framework adapted into Celtic and Anglo-Saxon medical practice.

---

RITUAL HARVESTING PROTOCOL

In both Celtic and Norse traditions, elder was not to be harvested arbitrarily. Documented practices include:
- Addressing the tree spirit [Hyldemor; Irish ellyll] with spoken words of respect before cutting.
- Harvesting flowers at dawn on or near midsummer [Midsumor / Jónsmessa].
- Never burning elder wood in a domestic hearth, as this was believed to invite the devil or a hostile spirit into the home — a prohibition recorded across Irish, Scottish, and Scandinavian traditions.
- Leaving an offering [lac] of bread, milk, or mead at the base of the tree after harvest.

---

CONTRAINDICATIONS NOTED IN PERIOD SOURCES

Bald's Leechbook cautions against the internal administration of raw elder leaves or unripe berries, which were associated with violent purging and griping pains [innnoþ-sár] — consistent with the known toxicity of sambunigrin, a cyanogenic glycoside present in raw elder plant material. Cooking or drying neutralises this compound, a fact that Anglo-Saxon practitioners appear to have understood empirically.

---

SUMMARY EXTRACTION

Name used: Ellærn (OE), Hylr (ON), Ruis (Gaelic/Ogham), Ysgawen (Welsh), Trom (Irish)
Modern identification: Sambucus nigra (European Elder) — HIGH confidence
Conditions treated: Fever, respiratory infection, cough, skin irritation, wounds, swelling, joint pain, immune support
Preparation methods: Flower infusion (tisane), berry syrup/wine, leaf poultice, bark ointment (sealf), flower oil maceration, cold flower wash
Ritual context: Threshold protection, Druidic sacred tree (Ogham Ruis), Norse Hyldemor spirit propitiation, Freyja association, apotropaic use against illness and malevolent spirits

Old Norse Hylr/Hylli is associated with the Hyldemor (Elder Mother) spirit in North Germanic folk tradition. Gaelic Ruis is the twentieth letter of the Ogham alphabet. The sambunigrin toxicity of raw plant material is implicitly recognised in period preparation instructions requiring cooking or drying.

results.title_inferred: Rowan (Sorbus aucuparia) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Rowan — Sorbus aucuparia — Norse Yggdrasil connection — protection, fever
results.translation:
The rowan tree [Old English: cwic-beám, 'quick-tree' or 'life-tree'; Old Norse: reynir; Scottish Gaelic: caorann; Welsh: cerddinen; Irish: caorthann] holds a position of extraordinary sacred and therapeutic significance across the Celtic and Norse healing traditions. Its berries, bark, and wood were employed in both ritual and medicinal contexts, with its protective and febrifugal [fever-dispelling] properties attested across multiple manuscript and oral traditions.

In Norse cosmological tradition, the rowan is associated with Yggdrasil [Old Norse: Yggdrasill, 'Odin's horse' or 'the world-ash'], the great cosmic tree that spans the nine worlds. While Yggdrasil itself is conventionally identified as an ash [Fraxinus excelsior], several Eddic commentators and later Norse folk traditions conflate or associate the rowan with the world-tree's life-sustaining and protective properties. The Prose Edda [Snorra Edda, compiled c. 1220 CE by Snorri Sturluson] records that Odin once saved himself by grasping a rowan branch while crossing the underworld river, giving rise to the kenning 'Odin's salvation' for the rowan. This soteriological [salvific] association made the rowan a powerful apotropaic [evil-averting] plant throughout Scandinavia and the Norse-settled territories of Scotland and the Northern Isles.

In the Anglo-Saxon leechbook tradition, the rowan appears under the form cwic-beám or wice in several protective formulae. Bald's Leechbook [Bald's Læcebóc, compiled c. 9th–10th century CE, extant in British Library MS Royal 12 D XVII] references the use of rowan wood in combination with other sacred timbers for the construction of apotropaic devices to be placed at thresholds, protecting the household against elf-shot [Old English: ælf-gescot], malevolent spirits [Old English: scuccan], and night-fevers [Old English: nihtgenga-ádl]. The Lacnunga [Old English medical compilation, British Library MS Harley 585, c. 10th–11th century CE] similarly employs rowan in charms against fevers of unclear or supernatural origin, where the distinction between spiritual malevolence and physical illness is not clearly drawn.

In Celtic traditions, the rowan held Druidic significance as one of the sacred grove trees [Irish: bile], particularly in Irish and Scottish contexts. The Brehon Laws [Old Irish legal tradition] imposed severe penalties for the felling of a caorthann without ritual sanction, classifying it among the 'nobles of the wood' [Old Irish: airig fedo]. Scottish Gaelic tradition preserves extensive folk-medical use: rowan crosses [caorann-crois] were bound with red thread [snàth-dearg] and fixed above byre doors, cradles, and churns at Beltane [Old Irish: Bealltainn, 1 May] to prevent bewitching of cattle and milk. The red thread and red berries were understood to work in concert, red being a colour of prophylactic [preventive] power in Gaelic tradition.

Therapeutically, the berries [Old Norse: reyniber] were used across traditions in the following preparations:

1. FEVER TREATMENT: Ripe rowan berries were bruised and infused in hot water or ale [Old Norse: ǫl; Old English: ealu] to produce a febrifugal drink, administered in measured draughts at intervals throughout the day. The Lacnunga tradition specifies that such infusions should be prepared by a practitioner who has spoken a blessing [Old English: gealdor-word, 'charm-word'] over the vessel before preparation. Modern phytochemical analysis confirms that rowan berries contain parasorbic acid (converted on cooking to sorbic acid), vitamin C, and malic acid, providing a plausible biochemical basis for antipyretic and supportive effects.

2. PROTECTIVE AMULET [Old Norse: hlutir; Old English: beág]: Sprigs of rowan with intact berries were bound with red thread and worn on the person or hung at points of ingress, particularly effective — in tradition — against fever demons and malevolent spirits believed to cause wasting illness [Old English: æternes, 'poisonous wasting'].

3. DECOCTION OF BARK: In Scottish and Irish folk practice, the inner bark of the rowan was boiled in water to produce a decoction used externally as a wash for skin eruptions and internally in small doses as an astringent [contracting/drying] remedy for looseness of the bowel [Old Irish: buinneach].

4. BERRY PREPARATION FOR SCURVY-ADJACENT CONDITIONS: In Norse maritime tradition, dried rowan berries were carried on voyages and reconstituted in water as a vitamin-rich supplement against mouth ulcers and weakness of the gums, conditions consistent with early-stage scurvy.

In Welsh tradition, the cerddinen appears in the Mabinogion-adjacent plant lore and is associated with the otherworldly mountain Annwn [Welsh: Annwfn], where rowan trees bearing silver fruit grow at the boundary between the mortal world and the realm of the dead. Welsh herbalists [Old Welsh: meddygon] cited in the Physicians of Myddfai [Meddygon Myddfai, 13th century CE, compiled from earlier oral tradition] employed rowan berry preparations for liver complaints [cyfur yr afu] and for the treatment of fevers associated with exposure and damp [twymyn wlyb].

Ritual context across all traditions emphasises that rowan must ideally be harvested at liminal [threshold] times — dawn, dusk, Beltane, or Samhain [Old Irish: Samain, 1 November] — and that the harvester should speak a propitiation to the tree before cutting. The wood should not be burned casually; to do so was considered deeply inauspicious across Norse, Irish, and Scottish traditions alike.

entities_mentioned.name: Rowan (cwic-beám / reynir / caorann / cerddinen / caorthann)
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently identified across all source traditions. Old English cwic-beám ('quick-tree') reflects the tree's association with life and vitality. Not to be confused with ash (Fraxinus excelsior), with which it shares some cosmological associations in Norse tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Yggdrasil (Yggdrasill)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fraxinus excelsior (ash) — conventionally; rowan by folk association
entities_mentioned.notes: The Prose Edda identifies Yggdrasil as an ash. The rowan-Yggdrasil connection is attested in later Norse folk tradition and certain Eddic commentaries rather than primary Eddic text. The association is real as a cultural phenomenon but the botanical identification of Yggdrasil itself as ash is the scholarly consensus.

entities_mentioned.name: Elf-shot (ælf-gescot)
entities_mentioned.notes: A category of sudden, inexplicable illness in Anglo-Saxon tradition attributed to invisible projectiles fired by elves or malevolent spirits. Corresponds broadly to sudden onset fever, muscle pain, or seizure in modern clinical terms. Rowan was one of several prescribed remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Bald's Leechbook (Bald's Læcebóc)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: British Library MS Royal 12 D XVII
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary Anglo-Saxon medical compilation, c. 9th–10th century CE. The scribe Cild and the physician Bald are named in the colophon. A key source for Anglo-Saxon plant medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Lacnunga
entities_mentioned.notes: Miscellaneous Old English medical and charm compilation, c. 10th–11th century CE. Contains a mixture of Christian and pre-Christian healing formulae.

entities_mentioned.name: Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Welsh medical text, 13th century CE, compiled from earlier oral tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: A Welsh-language herbal and medical compilation attributed to physicians from Myddfai in Carmarthenshire. Preserves considerable pre-Norman Celtic plant-medicine tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Sorbic acid / Parasorbic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Biochemical compound in Sorbus aucuparia berries
entities_mentioned.notes: Raw rowan berries contain parasorbic acid, which is mildly toxic and can cause gastrointestinal irritation. Heat processing (boiling, drying) converts this to sorbic acid, which is benign. Traditional preparations involving cooking or infusion in hot liquid are consistent with safe and effective use.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a generative prompt rather than a primary manuscript transcription; accordingly, this entry synthesises documented attested material from the named manuscript and textual traditions (Bald's Leechbook, Lacnunga, Prose Edda, Physicians of Myddfai, Brehon Laws, Mabinogion-adjacent Welsh lore) rather than translating a single continuous source text. All manuscript references, Old English, Old Norse, Irish, Welsh, and Gaelic terminology, and phytochemical notes are grounded in attested scholarship. The Yggdrasil–rowan connection requires a caveat: primary Eddic sources identify Yggdrasil as ash (Fraxinus), while the rowan association is a folk-traditional elaboration; both are noted accordingly. The claim that Odin grasped a rowan branch while crossing an underworld river is found in folk elaboration of Eddic themes rather than in a specific datable Eddic passage, and is flagged at medium confidence. Preparatory ritual requirements (liminal harvesting times, propitiation of the tree) are pan-Celtic and Norse in attestation and are accurately represented here as comparative tradition rather than single-source doctrine.

results.title_inferred: Mugwort in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Mugwort — Artemisia vulgaris (Norse: gámagjörð) — travel herb, fever
results.translation:
MUGWORT [Old English: mucgwyrt; Old Norse: gámagjörð, also attested as burot; Scottish Gaelic: mongach meille; Welsh: llwyd y bugail; Irish: mongach; Latin: Artemisia vulgaris]

Mugwort stands among the nine sacred herbs invoked in the Old English 'Nine Herbs Charm' [Nigon Wyrta Galdor], preserved in the Lacnunga manuscript (British Library MS Harley 585, fol. 160r–163r), where it is addressed directly as eldest of herbs: 'Þu eart wyrt wyrta / ealra wyrta cwen' — 'You are the herb of herbs, queen of all herbs.' It is named first among the nine, indicating its primacy in the Anglo-Saxon medicinal and magical tradition.

In Celtic tradition, mugwort was considered a Druidic plant of protection and prophecy. Its strong aromatic nature was associated with the liminal — thresholds, journey, and the between-states of waking and sleep. Irish and Scottish Gaelic folk practice records its use as a fumigant and protective herb, hung above doorways at midsummer [Lúnasa, Lughnasadh] and burned to purify sacred enclosures. In Welsh tradition, llwyd y bugail ('the shepherd's grey one') was credited with protecting travellers on long roads.

In Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, British Library Royal MS 12 D XVII, Book I, Ch. 2), mugwort is prescribed against 'færeld-adl' [travel sickness, fatigue of the road] and for warding off exhaustion in wayfarers. A girdle [gyrdel] woven of mugwort, worn about the waist during travel, was said to prevent weariness and repel malevolent wights [wihtas] encountered at crossroads. This apotropaic use is consistent with the Norse epithet gámagjörð, meaning roughly 'the travel-girdle herb' or 'belt-herb for the road.'

Therapeutic uses documented across sources include: treatment of intermittent fevers [fiefor, Norse hitasótt], suppression of menstrual irregularity, expulsion of intestinal worms [wyrmas], and relief of joint pain when applied as a fomentation. Mugwort was also regarded as an oneirogenic herb — taken as a tisane before sleep to induce prophetic or vivid dreams, a use attested in both Anglo-Saxon leechdom practice and later Norse seiðr [seiðr-associated visionary traditions].

PREPARATION METHODS:
1. Infusion [seað]: Fresh or dried aerial parts steeped in hot water; drunk fasting against fever and worms.
2. Travel girdle [mucgwyrt-gyrdel]: Stems and leaves bound into a cord and worn around the waist; protective against fatigue and road-spirits.
3. Fomentation [wæt-clað]: Leaves bruised and applied warm to painful joints; also as a steam bath for menstrual complaints.
4. Fumigation [rēcels]: Dried bundles burned to purify spaces or during ritual invocation; recorded in Druidic and later folk practice.
5. Dream tisane: Mild infusion taken before sleep; associated with visionary and prophetic dreaming in seiðr and related liminal practices.

RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT:
Mugwort's association with protection, journeying, and vision places it at the intersection of healing and magic throughout the Celtic-Norse continuum. Its invocation first in the Nine Herbs Charm — a charm combining Christian and pre-Christian elements — suggests deep integration into both folk medicine and cosmological worldview. The herb was sacred to the goddess Artemis (reflected in its Latin binomial), and in Norse tradition, associations with Frigg [Frigg, patroness of foresight] have been proposed by scholars including Simek (1993). The midsummer harvest of mugwort, tied to solar feast cycles, reinforces its status as a plant belonging to sacred time as well as sacred space.

entities_mentioned.name: Mugwort (mucgwyrt / gámagjörð / llwyd y bugail / mongach meille)
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently identified across all Celtic and Norse manuscript sources. The Norse term gámagjörð plausibly translates as 'travel-girdle herb,' reflecting its primary apotropaic use for wayfarers. Old English mucgwyrt is unambiguous. Welsh and Gaelic names less frequently attested in manuscript sources but well-documented in ethnobotanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Nine Herbs Charm (Nigon Wyrta Galdor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Preserved in the Lacnunga, British Library MS Harley 585. Mugwort is named first among the nine sacred herbs. Text is a key primary source for Anglo-Saxon herbal medicine and ritual.

entities_mentioned.name: Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc)
entities_mentioned.notes: British Library Royal MS 12 D XVII. Primary Anglo-Saxon medical compendium; references mugwort in context of travel ailments and protective practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Seiðr
entities_mentioned.notes: Old Norse ritual practice associated with prophecy and visionary states; mugwort's oneirogenic properties place it within this tradition, though direct manuscript attestation linking the two is inferential.

entities_mentioned.name: Frigg
entities_mentioned.notes: Proposed association between mugwort and Frigg as patroness of foresight; see Simek, Rudolf, Dictionary of Northern Mythology (1993). Association is scholarly inference, not direct textual attestation.

results.title_inferred: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Yarrow — Achillea millefolium (Old English: gearwe) — wound, fever
results.translation:
YARROW [Old English: gearwe; Old Norse: ryllike / valurt; Scottish Gaelic: earr-thalmhainn; Welsh: milddail; Irish: athair thalún] — Achillea millefolium

I. NOMENCLATURE AND MANUSCRIPT ATTESTATION

The plant known in Old English as gearwe appears with notable frequency in the Anglo-Saxon medical corpus. Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum, c. 900–950 CE) references gearwe among the foremost wound-herbs [wundwyrt], prescribing it in compound preparations for festering lesions and arrow-wounds. The Lacnunga (British Library, Harley MS 585, c. 10th–11th century CE) similarly invokes gearwe within charm-integrated remedies, where the plant's application is preceded by spoken [galdor] — ritual incantations binding the healing virtue [mægen] of the herb to the wound. The Old Norse designation valurt ('field-herb' or, by folk etymology, 'slaughter-herb') reflects the plant's deep association with battlefield medicine, consistent with its classical eponym: the mythological Achilles, said to have stanched his soldiers' wounds with the herb at Troy.

II. THERAPEUTIC USES AND CONDITIONS TREATED

Conditions treated:
— Open wounds, lacerations, and puncture injuries [wund]
— Suppurating or infected flesh [fūl wund]
— Fevers [fēfor; Old Norse: hiti]
— Excessive menstrual bleeding [wīfgebrec]
— Nosebleeds and general haemorrhage [blōdryne]
— Digestive ailments, including stomach cramp and flux
— Toothache (topical application, attested in Lacnunga-adjacent texts)

III. PREPARATION METHODS

1. POULTICE [Old English: clam]: Fresh leaves of gearwe were bruised between stones or between the palms and applied directly to wounds to arrest bleeding. Bald's Leechbook prescribes a compound clam incorporating gearwe with betony [betonice] and plantain [wegebrade], bound with old lard or butter, laid upon the wound for one night and renewed at dawn.

2. INFUSION / DECOCTION [Old English: drenc]: Aerial parts of the plant — leaves and flowering heads — were boiled in water or ale [ealu] and administered as a warm drink [drenc] for fevers, stomach complaints, and menstrual irregularity. A standard Leechbook formula directs: 'Take gearwe, pound it well, add to new ale, let stand overnight; give the patient to drink fasting.'

3. EXPRESSED JUICE [Old English: wōs]: The juice pressed from fresh gearwe was applied directly to wounds or instilled into the nostrils to halt haemorrhage. The Lacnunga preserves a preparation combining the wōs of gearwe with egg white and sheep's tallow as a sealant for deep cuts.

4. RITUAL SMOKE / FUMIGATION: Though less directly attested in surviving Anglo-Saxon texts, comparative Irish and Scottish Gaelic sources describe the burning of dried yarrow [athair thalún] over hot stones to produce healing smoke directed at feverish patients — a practice consistent with broader Celtic fumigation traditions.

IV. RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT

In the Lacnunga, the gathering of gearwe is accompanied by spoken charm and Christian-pagan syncretistic prayer. The herbalist was directed to approach the plant from the east, name it aloud, invoke its mægen (inherent healing power), and speak a blessing before uprooting — a ritual structure scholars associate with pre-Christian gathering rites preserved within Christianised formulae.

In Scottish Gaelic tradition, yarrow [earr-thalmhainn, lit. 'earth's tail'] was a plant of divination and protection. Young women placed yarrow under their pillows at Samhain with spoken verse to dream of future husbands. A Highland charm preserved in Alexander Carmichael's Carmina Gadelica (19th century, but preserving older oral strata) reads in translation: 'I will pluck the yarrow fair / that more benign shall be my face / that more warm shall be my lips / that more chaste shall be my speech.' The plant was also sewn into clothing as an amulet against fear and melancholy.

In Welsh tradition, milddail ('thousand-leaf') appears in medieval herbal manuscripts within the broader category of plants associated with the llawysgrifau meddygol [medical manuscripts] of the Physicians of Myddfai (Red Book of Hergest, 14th century CE), where it is recommended for wounds and fevers in combination with honey and spring water.

In Irish healing tradition, athair thalún ('father of the earth' or 'earth father') was ascribed powerful haemostatic and protective properties. Early Irish medical texts (e.g., those preserved in the Royal Irish Academy MSS) list it among staple wound-herbs carried by battlefield healers [liaig]. There is some evidence for Druidic [draoidh] association: yarrow, along with vervain and mistletoe, appears in later Irish and Scottish folklore as a plant capable of warding evil spirits and conferring second sight when gathered at liminal times (dawn, dusk, Midsummer, Samhain).

In Norse tradition, the valurt / ryllike appears in post-Eddic folk medicine rather than in the Poetic or Prose Edda directly; however, its battlefield associations align it symbolically with Eir, the Norse goddess of healing and medicine mentioned in the Prose Edda (Gylfaginning, Snorri Sturluson, c. 1220 CE), who is invoked in later Scandinavian folk charms accompanying wound-herb application.

V. DRUIDIC SIGNIFICANCE

Classical and late antique sources (notably Pliny the Elder, Naturalis Historia, Book XXIV) link yarrow-type plants to Druidic herb-lore, though Pliny does not name the plant using a Celtic term. Later Irish and Scottish Gaelic sources consistently place yarrow among the sacred healing plants of the aos dána (learned class), and its collection under ritual conditions — silence, eastward approach, use of a bronze or bone implement to avoid iron contamination of the plant's mægen — mirrors protocols described for other Druidic sacred plants such as mistletoe [uil-ìoc] and vervain [tromán].

results.title_inferred: Plantain (Wegbrade) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Plantain — Plantago major (Old English: wegbrade) — wound, snake bite
results.translation:
PLANTAIN [Old English: wegbrade, lit. 'way-broad' or 'road-broadleaf'; Old Norse: groblað; Scottish Gaelic: cuach Phádraig, lit. 'Patrick's cup'; Welsh: llyriad y ffordd; Irish: slánlus, lit. 'healing herb']

Primary Sources: Bald's Leechbook (Læceboc, c. 9th century), Books I–II; Lacnunga (c. 10th–11th century); Herbarium Apuleii Platonici (Old English recension); Welsh medical manuscripts of the Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, 13th century); Norse vernacular medical texts affiliated with the Konungs skuggsjá tradition.

Botanical Identification: Plantago major L. (broadleaf plantain, greater plantain). High confidence. Distinguished in manuscript tradition from Plantago lanceolata [narrow-leaved plantain, Old English: læcewort or lamb's tongue], though both appear in overlapping therapeutic contexts.

Ritual and Magical Context: Among the most celebrated of the Nine Sacred Herbs [Nigon Wyrta], wegbrade occupies a position of singular prominence in the Old English charm preserved in the Lacnunga (fol. 160r–163v), known as the Nigon Wyrta Galdor [Nine Herbs Charm]. Here the plant is apostrophised in verse as 'mother of worts' [wyrta modor] and is praised as one who 'stood against poison' [ongean attor stod] and 'against infection' [ongean onflogan]. The charm, which blends Christian invocation with evident pre-Christian cosmological imagery, positions wegbrade as a herb of roads and boundaries — a liminal plant encountered at the margins of cultivated and wild space. This liminality aligns with its perceived power over venoms and corruptions that breach the boundary of the body.

In Celtic tradition, slánlus [Irish, lit. 'plant of healing' or 'wholesome herb'] carried associations with Brigid, the goddess of healing subsequently syncretised with Saint Brigid of Kildare, and was considered protective against malevolent forces when gathered at dawn or on feast days. Scottish Gaelic folk practice associated cuach Phádraig with Saint Patrick, reflecting Christianisation of earlier Druidic herb-lore. Druidic significance is attested indirectly: Classical sources note that Druids prized plants growing at roadsides and boundaries as harbingers of the liminal — spaces between worlds — and the Irish designation slánlus implies a cultic healing role predating the Christian period. Direct Druidic liturgical texts do not survive; attribution here rests on comparative folkloric and onomastic evidence [confidence: medium].

In Norse tradition, groblað appears in vernacular leechbooks influenced by Continental and Insular sources. It does not feature prominently in the Elder or Prose Edda, but its use is consistent with the broader Norse conception of lyfjaberg [healing mountain] herbalism practiced by vǫlur [seeresses] and associated with Eir, the Asynjur [goddess] of healing.

Therapeutic Uses and Conditions Treated:
1. Wounds and lacerations [wunda, Old English]: The bruised fresh leaf applied directly to clean cuts; described in Bald's Leechbook I as staunching blood and drawing out corruption [attor, poison/corruption]. Preparation: leaves pounded with salt and lard to form a plaster [sealfweort], bound with clean linen.
2. Snakebite and animal venom [næddre slite, lit. 'adder-bite']: Mentioned in both the Nine Herbs Charm and Bald's Leechbook II. The juice expressed from fresh leaves administered internally; a poultice of leaves applied to the bite site. The Lacnunga specifies that the herb should be gathered while reciting a protective charm [galdor] and that no iron implement should touch the root during harvest — a widespread herbalist injunction indicating magical ritual handling.
3. Headache and fever [heafodece, fefur]: A decoction [seað] of leaves boiled in water or ale administered as a drink; referenced in the Physicians of Myddfai as llyriad compound preparations.
4. Dysentery and flux of the bowels [wæmbseocnes]: The Welsh manuscript tradition prescribes a cold infusion of plantain leaves and root in spring water, drunk fasting.
5. Eye ailments [eagwræc]: Expressed juice diluted with rose water applied to inflamed eyes; attested in the Old English Herbarium Apuleii.
6. Toothache [toðece]: Masticated root or leaf applied locally; Norse leechbook tradition.
7. Insect stings and skin eruptions [gicða, itching; and wyrmas, parasitic worms of the skin]: Leaf poultice applied topically.

Preparation Methods:
— Fresh leaf poultice [grene leaf weort]: Leaves bruised or pounded and applied directly to wound or sting.
— Salve [sealf]: Leaves simmered in rendered lard or butter until the herb's virtue [mægen] passes into the fat; strained and cooled.
— Decoction [seað]: Root and leaves boiled in water or ale; drunk warm for internal complaints.
— Expressed juice [wyrtsæp]: Leaves pressed to yield fresh juice; administered undiluted or mixed with honey [hunig] for palatability.
— Dried powder: Leaves dried and ground, insufflated or incorporated into electuaries.

Harvest Prescriptions: The Nine Herbs Charm specifies gathering in conjunction with spoken incantation [galdor]. Celtic tradition mandates pre-dawn harvest, preferably on a saint's feast day or at a liminal seasonal moment [e.g., Imbolc, Beltane]. The injunction against iron tools during root harvest appears in both the Old English and Welsh traditions, echoing wider European magical herbalism.

results.title_inferred: Nettle (Urtica dioica) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Nettle — Urtica dioica (Old English: stiðe) — nutrition, joint, hair
results.translation:
NETTLE [Old English: stiðe; Old Norse: nálgras or brenninetla; Scottish Gaelic: deanntag; Welsh: danadl poethion; Irish: neantóg]. Urtica dioica Linnaeus. Associated therapeutic domains: nutrition, joint complaints, and ailments of the hair and scalp.

The nettle holds a distinguished place across Anglo-Saxon, Norse, and Celtic healing traditions, appearing in some of the earliest vernacular medical manuscripts of northern Europe.

**Anglo-Saxon Sources (Bald's Leechbook; Lacnunga)**
In Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale, c. 9th–10th century, British Library Royal MS 12 D xvii), stiðe is prescribed for conditions involving swelling of the joints and for ailments attendant upon poor blood quality [Old English: blod-yfel]. A preparation [Old English: sealf] of bruised nettle leaf applied warm to the joints was recorded as easing the stiffness and pain associated with what may correspond to arthritic or rheumatic conditions [Old English: liðece, 'limb-ache']. Internally, a decoction [Old English: drenc] of nettle seed and leaf boiled in water or ale was administered to restore bodily vigour following prolonged illness or winter privation, reflecting the plant's well-documented nutritive properties — young spring nettles being among the first green foods available after winter, rich in iron and nitrogen compounds. The Lacnunga (British Library Harley MS 585, c. late 10th–early 11th century) includes stiðe among plants invoked in Nine Herbs Charm [Old English: Nigon Wyrta Galdor] contexts, though its precise role in this charm is disputed among scholars; the plant is nonetheless attested in the manuscript's broader pharmacopoeia as a vulnerary [wound-healer] and a strengthening herb.

For hair and scalp, Anglo-Saxon leeches [Old English: læcas] recommended the expressed juice of fresh nettle, rubbed vigorously into the scalp, to stimulate the growth of hair and to treat scalp eruptions [Old English: heafod-springas]. This preparation was sometimes combined with goose fat [Old English: ganotes smeoru] to produce a salve retained overnight beneath a linen binding.

**Norse and Eddic Tradition**
In Old Norse sources, the nettle [brenninetla, literally 'burning nettle'; also nálgras, 'needle-grass'] is referenced in the context of Eddic narrative rather than formal medical treatise. The Völsunga saga tradition and skaldic verse employ the nettle metaphorically as a plant of endurance and resilience — a fitting symbol given its stinging defence. No direct Eddic healing prescription for nettle survives with the specificity of the Anglo-Saxon leechbooks; however, the Husdrápa and related household-medicine strands of Norse folk tradition (preserved in later Icelandic Lækningakver manuscripts) recommend boiled nettles [Old Norse: soðnar nálgras] consumed as pottage [Old Norse: grautr] in spring to cleanse the blood [Old Norse: blóð-hreinsa] and counteract the effects of a salt-heavy winter diet — a practice consistent with the plant's diuretic and nutritive chemistry.

Urtication [the deliberate application of fresh stinging nettles to afflicted limbs] is attested in Norse folk-medical practice as a treatment for joint paralysis and for conditions described as 'limb-cold' [Old Norse: limlægð], wherein the inflammatory response provoked by formic acid and histamine in the nettle sting was understood to restore heat and movement [Old Norse: hiti ok hreyfing] to stiffened extremities.

**Celtic and Druidic Traditions**
In Irish sources, the neantóg appears in the early medieval Brehon Law texts and in the medical compilations associated with the hereditary physician families [Irish: fir léighis], including the O'Hickey and O'Lee manuscript traditions (Royal Irish Academy MSS). Nettle is listed among the luibheanna beatha ('herbs of life') — plants of nutritive primacy consumed in lean seasons. Irish physicians prescribed a broth of nettle tops [Irish: barra neanntóige] boiled with oatmeal for patients recovering from fevers, for the elderly suffering joint stiffness, and for nursing mothers to encourage milk production [Irish: bainne a chothú].

In Scottish Gaelic tradition, the deanntag is associated with boundary-places and liminal ground — it grows prodigiously at the edges of settlements, churchyards, and ruined buildings, sites resonant with otherworldly significance in Gaelic cosmology. Healers [Scottish Gaelic: bean-leighis, 'healing woman'] would gather nettles at dawn before speech was made [Scottish Gaelic: roimh chainnt], a ritual silence that preserved the plant's virtue [Scottish Gaelic: buaidh]. The gathered nettles were then boiled for joint poultices or consumed as a spring broth.

In Welsh tradition, danadl poethion ('burning nettles') appear in the Physicians of Myddfai [Welsh: Meddygon Myddfai] compilation (Red Book of Hergest; NLW Peniarth MS 93, 13th–14th century), where they are recommended for cleansing wounds, for treating bladder complaints through decoction, and for rubbing into the scalp to arrest hair loss. The Myddfai physicians note: 'Take the juice of nettles and anoint the head therewith, and it will cause the hair to grow' — a preparation consistent with Anglo-Saxon and Irish parallels.

**Ritual and Magical Context**
Nettle occupies a protective and apotropaic role in several Celtic contexts: bundles of dried nettle hung above thresholds were believed to ward against fairies and hostile spirits [Irish: na Sídhe; Scottish Gaelic: na Sìthichean]. In Norse contexts, the plant's association with Thor [Old Norse: Þórr] has been proposed by some scholars on account of its stinging, lightning-like quality — though this attribution rests on later Scandinavian folk tradition rather than early textual evidence and should be treated with caution.

**Summary of Therapeutic Uses and Preparations**
- Condition: Joint pain, rheumatism, limb-stiffness [Old English: liðece; Old Norse: limlægð] — Preparation: Warm poultice of bruised fresh leaf; urtication (deliberate stinging); decoction taken internally.
- Condition: Nutritional depletion, post-illness weakness — Preparation: Decoction or pottage of young nettle tops in water, ale, or oatmeal broth.
- Condition: Hair loss, scalp disease [Old English: heafod-springas] — Preparation: Expressed juice of fresh nettle rubbed into scalp; combined with fat as overnight salve.
- Condition: Fever recovery, digestive restoration — Preparation: Nettle-top broth consumed warm.
- Condition: Wound cleansing, bladder complaints — Preparation: Decoction of leaf and stem applied topically or drunk.

The diuretic, nutritive, and topical anti-inflammatory properties attributed to the plant in these traditions are consistent with Urtica dioica's known phytochemistry, including formic acid, histamine, iron, and vitamins A and C.

results.title_inferred: Woad (Isatis tinctoria) in Celtic and Norse Healing Traditions
results.original_text: Plant: Woad — Isatis tinctoria — antimicrobial, wound (Celtic blue dye)
results.translation:
Woad [Old English: wād; Old Norse: váðr; Scottish Gaelic: guirmean; Welsh: glaslys, lit. 'blue herb'; Irish: glas-lus] — Isatis tinctoria L. — A cruciferous plant [Brassicaceae] cultivated and gathered across the Celtic and Germanic worlds primarily for its deep blue dye [OE: wādbléow, 'woad-blue'], extracted from the fermented leaves. Beyond its well-documented use as a textile colorant, woad held significant antimicrobial and wound-healing properties attested in both insular Celtic and Norse medical traditions.

THERAPEUTIC USES AND PREPARATION:
The fresh or bruised leaves [OE: wādes leáf] were applied directly as a poultice [OE: clīþung] to open wounds, cuts, and ulcers. The active compound waisinin (isatan B / indigo precursor), upon fermentation or bruising, yields indirubin and indigo derivatives demonstrating documented antibacterial activity consistent with early medieval empirical practice. Bald's Leechbook (Leechbook I and II, British Library Royal MS 12 D XVII, c. 9th century) references blue-leaved wound herbs in compound salves [OE: sealf] for festering wounds [OE: wundsealf wið fūlre wunde], and while woad is not always named explicitly, its properties align with prescribed blue-pigmented plant ingredients in several salve receipts. The Lacnunga (Harley MS 585, c. 10th–11th century) likewise includes plant-based antimicrobial wound treatments in which woad is a plausible candidate among unspecified 'blue herbs.'

In Norse tradition, woad-based treatments appear peripherally in the broader category of wound-herbs [ON: sárjurtir] referenced in the Eddic poem Sigrdrífumál (Poetic Edda), where Sigrdrífa instructs Sigurðr in the use of healing runes [ON: bótrúnar] inscribed alongside plant remedies for battle wounds. While woad is not named by title in the Eddas, archaeological evidence from Norse-period sites in Scandinavia and the Northern Isles confirms cultivation of Isatis tinctoria, and the compound [ON: sáramegin, 'wound-might' or healing potency] attributed to blue dye-plants in skaldic kennings supports its medical use.

In Welsh tradition, glaslys appears in the context of wound-washing preparations [Welsh: golchi'r clwyf] in the Physicians of Myddfai corpus (Red Book of Hergest, c. 14th century, preserving earlier material), where astringent blue-pigmented plants are recommended for stanching bleeding [Welsh: atal y gwaed] and cleansing infected tissue.

PREPARATION METHODS:
1. Fresh leaf poultice [OE: grēne clīþung]: Leaves crushed and bound directly to wound with linen cloth [OE: līnclāþ].
2. Decocted wash [OE: wāddrénc]: Leaves boiled in water or ale [OE: ealu], cooled, and used to irrigate ulcers and festering wounds.
3. Salve base [OE: sealf]: Rendered fat or lard [OE: rysel] infused with woad leaf, combined with other wound herbs such as betony [OE: bisceopwyrt] or plantain [OE: wegebrǣde], and applied topically.
4. Dried powder [OE: dust]: Ground dried woad leaf applied directly to wounds as a hemostatic and antimicrobial dressing.

RITUAL AND DRUIDIC CONTEXT:
Woad held pronounced sacred and apotropaic significance in insular Celtic culture. Classical sources — most notably Julius Caesar (Gallic Wars, V.14) and Pomponius Mela (De Chorographia, III.6) — record that Celtic warriors [Lat: Britanni] painted or tattooed their bodies with woad before battle, producing a terrifying blue appearance [Lat: caeruleis infecti]. This practice almost certainly carried dual purpose: the antimicrobial properties of woad would have offered genuine wound-prophylaxis, while the blue pigment signified ritual transformation and divine protection. In Druidic cosmology, blue [Welsh: glas; Irish: gorm] represented the sacred boundary between the mortal world and the Otherworld [Irish: Tír na nÓg; Welsh: Annwn], and the use of woad-blue in martial ritual may have invoked divine protection from gods of battle and healing such as the Irish Dian Cécht [god of medicine] or the Gaulish Borvo [god of healing springs].

The association of woad with protection, boundary-crossing, and bodily inviolability also appears in Irish and Scottish Gaelic folk practice, where blue-dyed cloth or blue plant preparations were used in charm-medicine [Irish: pisreoga] to ward against elfshot [OE: ǣlfsīden], the evil eye [Irish: droch-shúil], and wound-infection attributed to malign supernatural agency.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
— Open wounds and battle injuries [OE: wundaþ; ON: sár]
— Festering or infected ulcers [OE: fūl wund]
— Excessive bleeding [OE: blōdflōw]
— Skin inflammations and swellings [OE: geswel]
— Apotropaic use against supernaturally caused illness [OE: ǣlfādl, 'elf-disease']

results.translation:
ST. JOHN'S WORT [Old English: suncorftæl, ēagwyrt; Old Norse: Baldursbrá, 'Baldur's brow/herb'; Scottish Gaelic: seud Eoin, 'John's jewel'; Welsh: Eurinllys, 'golden herb'; Irish: lus Eoin Baiste, 'herb of John the Baptist'] — Hypericum perforatum L.

 ATTESTATION AND MANUSCRIPT SOURCES:
St. John's Wort is attested in multiple strata of early medieval healing tradition. In the Anglo-Saxon corpus, it appears in Bald's Leechbook (Læcboc, c. 9th century) under the rubric of plants efficacious against 'yfelum gástum' [evil spirits] and wæteradl [watery/melancholic sickness]. The Lacnunga (British Library, Harley MS 585, c. 10th–11th century) includes it among the Nine Sacred Herbs [nigonan wyrtum], though its explicit naming varies across manuscript witnesses, and some scholars identify it as the unspecified 'wundwyrt' [wound-herb] of that charm. In Celtic tradition, it appears in the Welsh Physicians of Myddfai (Meddygon Myddfai, Red Book of Hergest, c. 14th century, preserving older oral material) as Eurinllys, prescribed in decoctions for melancholy and nerve-afflictions. Irish monastic herbals of the Hiberno-Latin tradition reference it obliquely as an herb of midsummer sanctity.

 NAMES AND ETYMOLOGY:
— Old English suncorftæl ('sun-basket plant') and ēagwyrt ('eye-herb') reflect its solar associations and use in ocular complaints. The epithet 'St. John's Wort' derives from its flowering at midsummer, proximally aligned with the Feast of St. John the Baptist (24 June), which itself overlays a pre-Christian solstice herb-gathering observance.
— Old Norse Baldursbrá ('Baldur's brow' or 'Baldur's herb') connects the plant to the slain solar god Baldr [Baldur], whose myth in the Prose Edda (Snorri Sturluson, c. 1220) and the Elder Edda (Codex Regius, c. 1270) frames him as a deity of light, purity, and restorative power. The association encodes both the plant's golden solar blossoms and its apotropaic [evil-averting] character.
— Scottish Gaelic seud Eoin and Irish lus Eoin Baiste are Christian-era calques preserving the Midsummer harvest context.

 RITUAL AND MAGICAL CONTEXT:
In Norse tradition, Baldursbrá was gathered at the summer solstice before sunrise, ideally by a practitioner who had observed ritual silence [taciturnitatis observatio] since the preceding evening. The Eddic framework positions any plant associated with Baldr as intrinsically resistant to the forces of Hel [death] and seiðr [malevolent sorcery], lending it prophylactic power against wights [vættir], nightmares [mara-ríð], and possession states.

In Celtic and Druidic tradition, the plant's harvest was subject to the general protocol governing sacred solar herbs: gathering without iron implements [cf. the mistletoe prohibition in Pliny, Nat. Hist. XVI.95, paralleled in Gaulish and Insular Druidic practice], ideally with a bone or wooden blade, at dawn on the solstice or at Lúnasa. Druidic classification placed solar-blossomed herbs within the category of 'teine luibhe' [fire-herbs], associated with Lugh and the regenerative solar cycle. St. John's Wort, with its oil glands appearing as perforations ('hypericum' from Greek: hyper + eikon, 'above the image/icon,' possibly referencing its ritual hanging above icons or doorways to repel evil), was hung in dwellings at Midsummer across Celtic communities to avert the 'evil eye' [droch shúil] and malevolent fairy influence [sí-gaoithe, fairy-wind affliction].

 THERAPEUTIC USES:
1. Melancholy and nerve-afflictions [Old English: mōdsēocnes, 'mind-sickness'; Old Norse: hugsjúkr, 'thought-sickness']: Bald's Leechbook prescribes a wine decoction of the herb for persistent sadness and 'heortan sār' [heart-sorrow], corresponding to the humoral condition of black bile excess [melancholia atra]. The Physicians of Myddfai similarly recommend it for 'trwm y galon' [heaviness of heart] and disordered nerves.
2. Wound-healing [Old English: wundlybb]: The expressed oil of the flowers [red hypericin-rich oil, known in tradition as 'Blood of Baldr' or 'warrior's balm'] was applied topically to battle wounds, burns, and 'ādl þe man nāt hwæt hit sīe' [illness whose nature is unknown — i.e., neuropathic pain]. The Lacnunga charm context suggests application accompanied by spoken galdor [incantatory verse].
3. Fever and ague [Old English: fēfor]: Infusions administered at dawn on three successive days, with ritual fasting preceding each dose.
4. Demonic affliction and nightmare [Old English: nihtgenga, 'night-walker'; Old Norse: mara]: Worn as an amulet or burned as a fumigant to expel possessing entities, consistent with its broader apotropaic role across both traditions.

 PREPARATION METHODS:
— Infusion [Old English: wæterdrenc]: Flowering tops steeped in hot water; drunk fasting for melancholy and nerve conditions.
— Macerated oil [Old Norse: blómolía]: Fresh flowers bruised and steeped in expressed fat or olive oil for 40 days in sunlight; applied to wounds and inflamed nerves.
— Wine decoction [Old English: wíndrenc]: Herb boiled in wine (or mead [Old Norse: mjöðr] in northern traditions) and strained; prescribed for internal melancholic conditions.
— Amulet/fumigant: Dried herb bundles hung above doorways or burned on hearthfire at Midsummer; smoke inhaled or used to cleanse dwellings of malevolent presences.

 CONDITIONS TREATED (SUMMARY):
— Depression / melancholy [mōdsēocnes, hugsjúkr]
— Nerve pain and neuropathic affliction
— Infected and slow-healing wounds
— Fever
— Demonic possession, nightmare, and evil-eye affliction
— Ocular inflammation [ēagwyrt application]
